title: DAVID — Elizabeth I: The Tilbury Speech in Her Own English
logline: Biography of Elizabeth I with attested Tilbury speech line in Tudor English; sources on file.

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Royal Tongues — dead languages and the voices of history
brand.cta: Follow the Archive — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

sources.citation: Elizabeth I: Collected Works (University of Chicago Press) — Tilbury speech, 9 August 1588
sources.notes: Standard scholarly edition of attested speeches and letters

sources.citation: William Camden, Annales rerum Anglicarum et Hibernicarum (1615) — early transmission of Tilbury address
British Library, Armada collection — state papers and contemporary copies of Elizabeth's wartime addresses

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Tonight, Elizabeth the First — the queen who faced the Armada and left us her own words, attested on paper.
Born fifteen thirty-three, daughter of Anne Boleyn, crowned fifteen fifty-nine after half a sister's reign. Forty-four years on the throne — the long Elizabethan peace that was never quite peace.
Her world was Early Modern English in proclamation and prayer book, Latin in diplomacy, and a court culture that turned speech into propaganda when invasion threatened.
I know I have the body of a weak and feeble woman — but I have the heart and stomach of a king, and of a king of England too.
beats.speech_ipa: /aɪ nəʊ aɪ hæv ðə ˈbɒdi əv ə wiːk ənd ˈfiːbl ˈwʊmən/

beats.speech_text: She died in sixteen oh-three — no heir, Stuart succession, legend intact. What we can say attested is what she wrote and what her clerks copied.

lines: PERIOD LINE: Tilbury speech excerpt — attested text, reconstructed Early Modern pronunciation
PRONUNCIATION: Scholarly Early Modern English — not original audio
